partisan_flags.quote: Cabinet has deferred the bill through multiple parliamentary sessions since 2022
partisan_flags.why_biased: Asymmetric framing places perpetual blame on 'the government'/'Cabinet' without noting that ESSCOM was created under BN in 2013 and multiple administrations (BN then PH-led) have deferred the same bill; treats current unity government as uniquely responsible for 13-year structural failure
partisan_flags.fix: State explicitly: 'Deferred by successive Cabinets since 2022 across BN and PH-led administrations'

racial_flags.quote: many of Sulu, Bajau, or Filipino origin, born in Sabah but without citizenship. These communities predate the 2013 incursion but were securitised by it
racial_flags.why_biased: Assigns victimhood framing disproportionately to one ethnic-origin group (Sulu/Bajau/Filipino) while giving Sabah's 'own political class' agency and support for ESSCOM; treats these communities as passive objects of securitisation without equal scrutiny of cross-border security risks they are linked to
racial_flags.fix: Balance with: 'while cross-border security risks from the Sulu archipelago persist, local communities of Sulu/Bajau/Filipino origin face heightened scrutiny'

geographic_flags.quote: Sabah also has unresolved grievances with Kuala Lumpur: infrastructure gaps, development delays, and the sense that eastern Malaysia's security needs are treated as afterthoughts until a crisis forces attention
geographic_flags.why_biased: Explicitly frames Peninsular KL as the neglectful centre and Eastern Sabah as the aggrieved periphery; this is the opposite of default KL-centric bias but still constitutes strong geographic skew by making East Malaysia's perspective the unchallenged moral default
geographic_flags.fix: Add Peninsular perspective: 'Sabah leaders argue... while federal planners cite competing national priorities'

economic_flags.quote: Additional costs of RM28.7 million pushed the total to RM81.51 million — nearly double the original budget
economic_flags.why_biased: Highlights contractor failure and cost doubling purely as governance scandal without any economic context on why public infrastructure projects in remote Sabah routinely face such overruns (supply chain, labour, security premiums); masks potential class/regional economic realities behind 'government failure' narrative
economic_flags.fix: Note: 'Cost overruns common in remote East Malaysian projects due to logistics and security factors'

omission_flags: Completely omits any Sabah political party perspective (GRS, STAR, WARISAN, PH Sabah) despite noting 'Sabah's own political class unanimously supports ESSCOM' — their specific demands or criticisms of federal handling are missing, erasing local political agency
Ignores B40 and coastal community economic impact: zero mention of how the 13-year nighttime curfew affects fishermen livelihoods or tourism-dependent B40 in ESSZone
No youth or women's perspective on living under permanent 'temporary' security measures in a region with high undocumented population
Unused 'Economic' and 'Social' lenses leave unexamined whether infrastructure delays are due to federal underfunding or Sabah state implementation capacity
evasion_flags: Dodges direct conclusion on why no Act of Parliament in 13 years despite multiple plans and budgets: analysis stops at 'deferred' without confronting whether this reflects deliberate federal control preference over Sabah or genuine legislative gridlock
Pivots to 'pragmatic' MOB concept as admission of failure without assessing if mobile platforms are actually superior or just cheaper political theatre
shoe_test_failures: If the analysis swapped 'PH-led unity government' for 'BN government' in the deferral/cost sections, the critical tone would read as opposition attack; current version avoids naming any party yet implies current government culpability

If the undocumented communities were described as 'Malay/Muslim settlers from Peninsular' instead of Sulu/Bajau/Filipino, the victimhood framing would immediately trigger racial bias alarms — reveals subtle ethnic skew
overall_assessment: The analysis is mostly factual and operationally fair but carries a clear anti-federal-government tilt that treats 'the government' as perpetually negligent on institutional permanence while soft-pedalling Sabah's own governance role and East Malaysian ethnic complexities. Strong geographic pro-Sabah peripheral focus is refreshing but unbalanced, and the undocumented communities receive mild victim framing without equal security-risk acknowledgment. Overall low-to-moderate bias that leans mildly opposition/governance-critical without overt partisanship or racism, but omissions on local politics, economic context and B40 impacts weaken its claim to comprehensive maturity.
_note: Grok returned bias-audit format instead of courage/stress-test format. CT score derived from evasion flags and overall critique: estimated 55.
